mnemonics.title: KILL Syntax
mnemonics.content: KILL(pid, sig) - Remember as 'KILL Process ID with Signal Number' - pid identifies WHO, sig identifies WHAT

acronyms.expansion: Keep Interprocess Linkage Link - Sends signals between processes
acronyms.usage: Used to send signals to processes for termination, interruption, or communication

acronyms.expansion: Request Action In Self Environment - Process sends signal to itself
acronyms.usage: Used when process needs to handle its own signal, like graceful termination

acronyms.expansion: Error: No Such Resource - ESRCH errno when PID not found
acronyms.usage: Checked when kill() fails to find target process

acronyms.expansion: Error: PERMission denied - EPERM errno when permission issues
acronyms.usage: Checked when kill() fails due to permission restrictions

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box split into two: left side 'pid' pointing to target process (WHO), right side 'sig' showing signal type (WHAT)
Draw process A pointing arrow to process B labeled 'kill(pid, sig)', and process A pointing arrow to itself labeled 'raise(sig)'
Number line: 1-31 with SIGKILL=9 highlighted in red (forced), SIGTERM=15 in yellow (graceful), others in green

analogies.analogy: Like a phone call - you dial a number (pid) and choose message type (sig) to send to another person
Like talking to yourself in a mirror - the sender and receiver are the same person (process)
SIGKILL is like pulling the plug (immediate), SIGTERM is like asking nicely to leave (graceful)
Like an alarm going off - you decide whether to handle it (custom handler), ignore it (SIG_IGN), or use default (SIG_DFL)
